MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(loc:Property)
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.idShort, loc.idShort, loc.value

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN aas.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, asset.assetKind, asset.assetType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(parent:Entity)
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(child:Entity)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN aas.idShort, parent.idShort, child.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, asset.assetKind

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(leaf:Entity)
WHERE NOT (leaf)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->()
MATCH (leaf)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN DISTINCT leaf.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, asset.assetKind
ORDER BY leaf.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(leaf:Entity)
WHERE NOT (leaf)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->()
MATCH (leaf)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)-[:HAS_SPECIFIC_ASSET_ID]->(sai:SpecificAssetId)
RETURN DISTINCT leaf.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, sai.name, sai.value
ORDER BY leaf.idShort

MATCH (asset:Asset)
WHERE asset.globalAssetId IN ['urn:asset:mir250:001', 'urn:asset:mir250:002', 'urn:asset:ur20:001', 'urn:asset:ur3e:001']
RETURN asset.globalAssetId, asset.assetKind, asset.assetType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/nameplate/3/0/Nameplate'})
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (aas)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN aas.idShort AS hallName, aas.id AS aasId, asset.globalAssetId, asset.idShort AS deviceId
ORDER BY aas.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(element)
RETURN sm.idShort, element.idShort, labels(element)

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN sm.idShort, entity.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, asset.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)
OPTIONAL MATCH (entity)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(prop:Property)
WHERE entity.idShort IN ['UR20_001', 'MiR250_002', 'UR3e_001', 'MiR250_001']
RETURN entity.idShort, prop.idShort, prop.value, prop.valueType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
OPTIONAL MATCH (asset)-[:HAS_SPECIFIC_ASSET_ID]->(sai:SpecificAssetId)
WHERE entity.idShort IN ['UR20_001', 'MiR250_002', 'UR3e_001', 'MiR250_001']
RETURN entity.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, sai.name, sai.value

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
OPTIONAL MATCH (asset)-[:HAS_SPECIFIC_ASSET_ID]->(sai:SpecificAssetId)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE entity.idShort IN ['UR20_001', 'MiR250_002', 'UR3e_001', 'MiR250_001']
RETURN entity.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, sai.name, sc.id AS semanticId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort IN ['HierarchicalStructures', 'Technical_Data']
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
OPTIONAL MATCH (asset)-[:HAS_SPECIFIC_ASSET_ID]->(sai:SpecificAssetId)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE entity.idShort IN ['UR20_001', 'MiR250_002', 'UR3e_001', 'MiR250_001']
RETURN entity.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, sm.idShort, sai.name, sc.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'Nameplate'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(collection:SubmodelElementCollection)

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)

MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(prop:Property {idShort: 'ManufacturerProductDesignation'})

WHERE sm.idShort = 'Nameplate'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(el)

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)

MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(el)
WHERE el.idShort IN ['ModelName', 'ManufacturerName', 'ManufacturerProductDesignation', 'SerialNumber', 'YearOfConstruction', 'URIOfTheProduct']
MATCH (el)-[:HAS_VALUE]->(val:LangString)
RETURN sm.idShort, el.idShort, val.language, val.text

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)

RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, el.value

RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.id

WHERE sm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(parent:Entity)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(child:Entity)
OPTIONAL MATCH (child)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN hall.idShort, parent.idShort, child.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, child.entityType

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)

MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)
WHERE entity.idShort IN ['UR20_001', 'MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002', 'UR3e_001']
OPTIONAL MATCH (entity)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN entity.idShort, entity.entityType, asset.globalAssetId, asset.assetKind

MATCH (e:Entity)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(child:Entity)
WHERE child.entityType = 'Robot'

RETURN e.idShort AS container, child.idShort AS robot, asset.globalAssetId, asset.assetKind
ORDER BY e.idShort, child.idShort

MATCH (e:Entity)
WHERE e.entityType CONTAINS 'Robot'
RETURN e.idShort, e.entityType, e.smId

MATCH (a:Asset)
WHERE a.assetType IS NOT NULL
RETURN a.globalAssetId, a.assetType, a.assetKind
ORDER BY a.assetType

MATCH (a:Asset)
RETURN a.globalAssetId, a.assetType, a.assetKind
ORDER BY a.assetType, a.globalAssetId

MATCH (a:Asset)
WHERE a.globalAssetId CONTAINS 'mir' OR a.globalAssetId CONTAINS 'hall'
RETURN a.globalAssetId, a.assetType, a.assetKind
ORDER BY a.globalAssetId

Based on my investigation, I found that the AAS graph contains information about the autonomous transport robots (MiR series AGVs), but their current physical locations are not stored in the AAS repository. Let me provide you with what I found:

## Autonomous Transport Robots Found

I found **3 autonomous transport robots** (AGVs) in the AAS environment:

| Robot ID | Type | Serial Number | Manufacturing Date |
|----------|------|---------------|-------------------|
| **MiR100_001** | MiR100 Type | MIR100-2020-001 | 2020-04-12 |
| **MiR250_001** | MiR250 Type | MIR250-2023-001 | 2023-08-15 |
| **MiR250_002** | MiR250 Type | MIR250-2023-002 | 2023-09-20 |

### Robot Specifications (from TechnicalDataAGV submodel):
- **MiR100**: Smaller AGV for indoor logistics
- **MiR250**: Larger AGV for heavier loads

### Location Information

**The current physical locations of these robots are NOT stored in the AAS graph.** 

However, the facility infrastructure includes:
- **Hall3**: 8,000 m² area with 10 kW power connection
- **Hall4**: 12,000 m² area with 10 kW power connection

The MiR robots have technical data submodels with information about their capabilities (navigation type, localization type, vehicle dimensions, max load, speed, etc.), but no current location tracking data is present in the AAS repository.

### Recommendation

To track robot locations, you would need to:
1. Add an **AssetLocation** submodel (template: `Data Model for Asset Location`) to each instance AAS
2. Or use the existing **FacilityInformation** submodels to register which hall each robot is assigned to
3. Or implement real-time location tracking via OPC UA or other sensors

Would you like me to help you set up location tracking for these robots?

Based on my investigation, I found that the AAS graph contains information about the autonomous transport robots (MiR series AGVs), but their current physical locations are not stored in the AAS repository. Let me provide you with what I found:

## Autonomous Transport Robots Found

I found **3 autonomous transport robots** (AGVs) in the AAS environment:

| Robot ID | Type | Serial Number | Manufacturing Date |
|----------|------|---------------|-------------------|
| **MiR100_001** | MiR100 Type | MIR100-2020-001 | 2020-04-12 |
| **MiR250_001** | MiR250 Type | MIR250-2023-001 | 2023-08-15 |
| **MiR250_002** | MiR250 Type | MIR250-2023-002 | 2023-09-20 |

### Robot Specifications (from TechnicalDataAGV submodel):
- **MiR100**: Smaller AGV for indoor logistics
- **MiR250**: Larger AGV for heavier loads

### Location Information

**The current physical locations of these robots are NOT stored in the AAS graph.** 

However, the facility infrastructure includes:
- **Hall3**: 8,000 m² area with 10 kW power connection
- **Hall4**: 12,000 m² area with 10 kW power connection

The MiR robots have technical data submodels with information about their capabilities (navigation type, localization type, vehicle dimensions, max load, speed, etc.), but no current location tracking data is present in the AAS repository.

### Recommendation

To track robot locations, you would need to:
1. Add an **AssetLocation** submodel (template: `Data Model for Asset Location`) to each instance AAS
2. Or use the existing **FacilityInformation** submodels to register which hall each robot is assigned to
3. Or implement real-time location tracking via OPC UA or other sensors

Would you like me to help you set up location tracking for these robots?

## Autonomous Transport Robots

We have **2 types** of autonomous transport robots (AGVs) with **3 instances** in the system:

### 1. **MiR100 Type** (Carrier AGV)
- **Product:** MiR100
- **Dimensions:** 580mm × 890mm × 352mm (W×L×H)
- **Max Load:** 100 kg
- **Weight:** 70 kg
- **Navigation:** Free navigation with Laser SLAM
- **Protection:** IP21
- **Kinematics:** Differential drive
- **Max Speed:** 1.5 m/h (empty), 1.0 m/h (with max load)
- **Max Runtime:** 10 hours

**Instances:**
- **MiR100_001** (AAS ID: urn:aas:mir100:001)
  - Serial Number: MIR100-2020-001
  - Year of Construction: 2020
  - Date of Manufacture: 2020-04-12

### 2. **MiR250 Type** (Carrier AGV)
- **Product:** MiR250
- **Dimensions:** 910mm × 1350mm × 322mm (W×L×H)
- **Max Load:** 250 kg
- **Weight:** 215 kg
- **Navigation:** Free navigation with Laser SLAM
- **Protection:** IP52
- **Kinematics:** Differential drive
- **Max Speed:** 2.0 m/h (empty), 1.5 m/h (with max load)
- **Max Runtime:** 15 hours

**Instances:**
- **MiR250_001** (AAS ID: urn:aas:mir250:001)
- **MiR250_002** (AAS ID: urn:aas:mir250:002)

### Location Information

**Important:** The AAS data does **not contain specific location information** for these robots. While the system has:
- FacilityInformation submodels for Hall3 and Hall4
- An AssetLocation template available

result.query: Which autonomous transport robots do we have and where are they located?
result.response:
I'll help you find the autonomous transport robots and their locations. Let me start by gathering the necessary information about templates and the graph structure.









Good. I found the relevant templates:
- **"Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles"** (semanticId: `https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0`) - for AGV/transport robots
- **"Data Model for Asset Location"** (semanticId: `https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0`) - for location data
- **"FacilityInformation"** (semanticId: `https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0`) - for facility/hall information

Now let me query the graph to find all AAS with AGV/transport robot technical data submodels and their locations.









I found 2 AGV type AAS (MiR250_Type and MiR100_Type). Now let me find the instance shells derived from these types, and also check for location data. Let me query for all instances and their locations.









I found 3 instances but the location data isn't showing up in the techDataSm. Let me search more broadly for location submodels in these AAS to see if there's a separate location submodel.









The results show the AAS have Nameplate and ServiceRequestNotification submodels, but no TechnicalDataAGV or AssetLocation submodels visible for the instances. Let me check the FacilityInformation template to see if locations are stored there, and also search for any submodels with location-related semantic IDs.









Good - there are FacilityInformation submodels for Hall3 and Hall4. Let me check if the AGV instances are linked to these halls through relationships or if there's another way location is stored. Let me also search for any Property elements that might contain location data.









Let me try a different approach - search for all properties across the AGV instances and also check if there's a Location submodel that might be missing from the submodel list.









I see the AGV instances have Nameplate and ServiceRequestNotification submodels, but no explicit location submodel. Let me check if the MiR250_001 and MiR250_002 have any additional submodels, and also search for any submodel that might contain location data across all shells.









Let me check the Hall3 and Hall4 FacilityInformation submodels to see what location data they contain, and also search for any AssetLocation semantic ID submodels in the system.









The FacilityInformation submodel contains power connection and area data, but not specific asset locations. Let me search for any submodels with the AssetLocation semantic ID across all shells, and also check if there's a relationship between the AGV instances and the halls.









No AssetLocation submodels exist. Let me check if there are any relationships between the AGV instances and the halls, or if location is stored elsewhere.









Interesting - there's a match but the relationship is null. Let me try a different approach - search for any element in the AGV instances that might contain location data, or check if there are Property elements at any level.









The properties found are mostly from ServiceRequestNotification submodels (service requests) and Nameplate. There's no explicit location data in these submodels. Let me check if there are any other submodels or if location is stored in a different way. Let me also search for all AAS shells to get a complete picture.









Good - I can see the complete list. The autonomous transport robots (AGVs) are:
- MiR100_001 (instance of MiR100_Type)
- MiR250_001 (instance of MiR250_Type)
- MiR250_002 (instance of MiR250_Type)

The halls are Hall3 and Hall4. Let me check if there are any FacilityInformation submodels that might indicate which halls the AGVs are located in, or if there's any relationship data I'm missing.









Let me check Hall4 as well, and then search for any Entity elements that might represent assets and their locations.









The FacilityInformation submodels contain power connection and area data, but not specific AGV locations. Let me search for any Entity elements that might represent assets in specific halls, or check if there's any other way location is stored.









Based on my comprehensive search of the AAS graph, I have found the autonomous transport robots but no explicit location data is stored in the system. Let me provide you with the findings:

## Autonomous Transport Robots (AGVs) Found

According to the graph data, we have **3 autonomous transport robots**:

| Robot Name | Type | ID |
|------------|------|-----|
| MiR100_001 | MiR100_Type | urn:aas:mir100:001 |
| MiR250_001 | MiR250_Type | urn:aas:mir250:001 |
| MiR250_002 | MiR250_Type | urn:aas:mir250:002 |

## Location Information

**No location data is currently stored in the AAS repository.** 

The available data shows:
- The robots have **Nameplate** submodels (with serial numbers and manufacturing data)
- The robots have **ServiceRequestNotification** submodels (containing service requests)
- There are **FacilityInformation** submodels for Hall3 (8000 sqm, 10 kW) and Hall4 (12000 sqm, 10 kW), but these don't contain specific robot location assignments
- No AssetLocation submodels exist in the system
- No explicit relationships between the AGV instances and the halls are stored

**Note:** The FacilityInformation submodels indicate the factory has Hall3 (8000 m²) and Hall4 (12000 m²), but the autonomous transport robots do not have explicit location assignments stored in their AAS.

If you need to track robot locations, you would need to either:
1. Add AssetLocation submodels to each robot's AAS
2. Create explicit relationships between the robot AAS and the hall AAS
3. Store location data in the Nameplate or TechnicalData submodels

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(loc:SubmodelElement)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})
RETURN aas.idShort AS aasName, aas.id AS aasId, sm.idShort AS techDataSm, loc.idShort AS locElement

MATCH (instance:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:DERIVED_FROM]->(type:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE type.idShort IN ['MiR250_Type', 'MiR100_Type']
OPTIONAL MATCH (instance)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(loc:SubmodelElement)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})
RETURN instance.idShort AS instanceName, instance.id AS instanceId, type.idShort AS typeName, sm.idShort AS techDataSm, loc.idShort AS locElement, loc.value AS location
ORDER BY instance.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ['MiR100_001', 'MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sc.id AS semanticId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
RETURN aas.idShort AS aasName, sm.idShort AS smName, sm.id AS smId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE el.modelType = 'Property'
RETURN sm.idShort AS submodelName, el.idShort, el.value, el.valueType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN sm.idShort AS submodelName, el.idShort, el.smId, el.modelType
LIMIT 20

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR250_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'FacilityInformation'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.value, el.valueType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (agv:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})
MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})
MATCH (hall2:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall4'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (agv)-[r]->(hall)
OPTIONAL MATCH (agv)-[r2]->(hall2)
RETURN agv.idShort AS agvName, hall.idShort AS hallName, r AS relationship, r2 AS relationship2

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property)
RETURN sm.idShort, p.idShort, p.value, p.valueType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, aas.category
ORDER BY aas.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'FacilityInformation'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.value, el.valueType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall4'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'FacilityInformation'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.value, el.valueType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(e:Entity)
RETURN sm.idShort, e.idShort, e.entityType

According to the MiR250 operator manual (found in the HandoverDocumentation submodel), here's what it says about emergency stops:

## Emergency Stop Key Information

### ⚠️ Important Notice
**"Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use."** (p. 85)

### Emergency Stop Circuit
The Emergency stop circuit goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and connects to the top module. Any number of Emergency stop buttons can be connected to the circuit. When the circuit is broken, the robot goes into Emergency stop. (p. 86)

### Status Indicators
- **Blinking blue light**: The robot is waiting for a user action to clear the Emergency stop state and acknowledge the change of operating mode. (p. 18)

### Shared Emergency Stop Interface
The robot has shared emergency stop inputs/outputs on the Auxiliary safety function interfaces:
- **Pin 3**: Shared emergency stop in (Input) - Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- **Pin 6**: Shared emergency stop out (Output) - Inactive when the robot is in emergency stop (p. 98)

The shared emergency stop function works in three cases:
1. Robot not in emergency stop (24V output)
2. Robot in emergency stop due to 0V input from Shared emergency stop interface
3. Robot in emergency stop because the inputs are unequal (p. 98)

### Troubleshooting Emergency Stop
When the robot enters Emergency stop due to a safety system component, you can identify the issue by:
- Signing in to the robot interface
- Going to **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system** to view displayed errors (p. 99)

### Hardware Error Resolution
If you encounter hardware errors:
1. Check that the **Emergency stop button is released**
2. Turn the robot off and on again to reset components
3. Check for physical damage to the robot, 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels
4. If an internal component is failing, disconnect the battery and have the commissioner or operator visually check it (p. 209)

### Color Indications
The status lights turn **red** when the robot is in Protective stop (which can occur during emergency conditions). When the robot is waiting for user action to clear emergency stop, the lights **blink blue** (p. 84, p. 18).

**Source:** MiR250 User Guide (en), Revision v.1.1, Date: 07/2020, Mobile Industrial Robots A/S

First, let me find which AAS contains the MIR 250 asset and its associated documentation.

Found the MIR 250 assets. Now let me get the PDF documents for the MIR 250 shells. I'll check the Type AAS first since documentation typically lives there:









Found the MIR 250 manual. Now let me search for emergency stop information in the PDF:









The submodel ID seems wrong. Let me check the correct submodel that contains the PDF file:









Now let me search the correct submodel:









Good, I found some emergency stop information. Let me search for more specific information about emergency stops to get a complete picture:









Let me search for more specific emergency stop information to get comprehensive details:









Based on the operator manual search results, I can now provide you with comprehensive information about emergency stops for the MiR 250:

## Emergency Stop Information - MiR 250 Operator Manual

**Important Notice (Page 85):**
> "Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use."

### Emergency Stop Circuit (Page 86)
- The Emergency stop circuit goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and connects to the top module
- Any number of Emergency stop buttons can be connected to the circuit
- **When the circuit is broken, the robot goes into Emergency stop**

### Shared Emergency Stop (Page 87)
- This function consists of a shared circuit between the robot and top module
- Enables them to trigger each other into an Emergency stop

### Emergency Stop Indications (Page 18)
- **Blinking blue**: The robot is waiting for a user action (clear the Emergency stop state, acknowledge the change of operating mode)

### Manual Stop vs Emergency Stop (Page 17)
- **Manual stop**: Pressing this button stops the robot. After pressing, you must press the Resume button to let the robot continue operating
- Color indication: Red means it is possible to engage Manual stop

### Diagnostics (Page 99)
When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a component in the safety system, you can identify which component is causing the issue by:
1. Signing in to the robot interface
2. Viewing errors under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**

### Troubleshooting Emergency Stop (Page 99 - Common Issues)
If the robot enters Emergency stop due to a safety system component, check the safety system errors in the robot interface to identify exactly which component is causing the issue.

### Safeguarded Stop (Page 95)
- The safeguarded stop interface provides an input that can bring the robot into **Protective stop** (different from Emergency stop)
- Uses two input pins - both must receive 24V for the robot to operate
- If either pin receives 0V, the robot enters Protective stop
- If pins are unequally set for more than 3 seconds, the safety PLC registers an error and needs to be reset by restarting the robot

**Source:** MiR250 User Guide (en), Date: 07/2020, Revision: v.1.1, from the HandoverDocumentation submodel

I need to find the operator manual for the MiR 250 and search for emergency stop information. Let me start by discovering what AAS shells exist and their submodels to locate the documentation.









Let me search more broadly for MiR 250 assets:









Let me check the cypher manual for the correct pattern and also search for all AAS shells to find the MiR 250:









Let me list all AAS shells to find the MiR 250:









Found it! There's a MiR250_Type at `urn:aas:mir250:type`. Let me get the submodels for this type shell:









I see there's a "HandoverDocumentation" submodel which likely contains the operator manual. Let me search for PDF files in that submodel and also search the documents for emergency stop information:









I found several relevant results about emergency stops. Let me search for more specific information about emergency stop procedures:









Let me search for more specific emergency stop procedures and also check the troubleshooting page:









Now let me compile the information I've gathered from the search results. I have good information about emergency stops from the MiR 250 operator manual. Let me provide a comprehensive answer:

---

Based on the MiR 250 operator manual found in the HandoverDocumentation submodel, here's what it says about emergency stops:

## Emergency Stop Usage (Page 85)
**Important Notice:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.

## Emergency Stop Indicator (Page 18)
When the emergency stop is activated, the robot shows a **blinking blue** status light, indicating:
- The robot is waiting for a user action to clear the Emergency stop state
- The user needs to acknowledge the change of operating mode

## Troubleshooting Emergency Stop (Page 209)
When troubleshooting hardware errors, the manual states:
- **Check that the Emergency stop button is released**
- If the error persists after releasing the button, continue with other checks

## Maintenance Checks (Page 178)
Under Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop, you can check if:
- Front scanner cover is Clean
- Back scanner cover is Clean

## Common Issues (Page 99)
When the robot enters **Emergency stop** due to a component in the safety system, you can identify exactly which component is causing the issue by:
- Signing in to the robot interface
- Viewing the errors displayed under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**

## Protective Stop vs Emergency Stop (Page 84)
The manual distinguishes between Protective stop and Emergency stop. In Protective stop:
- The robot's status lights turn **red**
- Internal safety contactors are switched off
- Top application and all moving parts do not receive power
- You can hear audible clicks when safety contactors switch

## Next Steps
To resolve an emergency stop situation:
1. Identify the cause via the robot interface (Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system)
2. Release the emergency stop button
3. Acknowledge the state change (the robot will wait for this action)
4. The robot will resume normal operation

For further assistance, contact MiR Technical Support at support@mir-robots.com or call +45 20 377 577.

result.response:
I need to find the AAS for the MiR 250 robot and then search its documentation for emergency stop information.

Let me start by querying the graph to find the MiR 250 asset and get its submodel ID, then search the documentation.









I need to use exact matching. Let me first get the templates index and then query properly. Let me also check the manual page for cypher patterns.









Now let me query the graph to find the MiR 250 AAS using exact matching. Let me try a broader search first to see what AAS shells exist:









Found the MiR250 AAS shells. Now let me get the submodels and their PDF documents for one of the MiR250 instances. I'll start with the type shell (which typically contains the documentation):









Found the MiR250 PDF document. Now let me search for emergency stop information in the MiR250 manual:









Good, I found some relevant results. Let me search for more specific information about emergency stop procedures and activation:









Let me search for more specific information about clearing emergency stop and troubleshooting:









Now I have comprehensive information from the MiR250 operator manual about emergency stops. Let me compile the answer for the user.

---

## MiR250 Operator Manual - Emergency Stop Information

According to the MiR250 operator manual (found in the AAS documentation), here's what it says about emergency stops:

### **What is Emergency Stop?**
Emergency stop is a state the robot enters when an **Emergency stop button has physically been pressed**. When in Emergency stop:
- The status light turns **red**
- You cannot move the robot or send it on missions
- **To recover**: Release the Emergency stop button and then press the **Resume button**

### **Emergency Stop Circuit**
The Emergency stop circuit goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and connects to the top module. Multiple Emergency stop buttons can be connected to this circuit. When the circuit is broken, the robot goes into Emergency stop.

### **Shared Emergency Stop (Page 97)**
The Shared emergency stop interface allows the robot and top module to control Emergency stop state together:
- **Inputs**: Two inputs for bringing the robot into Emergency stop
- **Outputs**: Two outputs signaling when the robot is in Emergency stop
- When operational: outputs deliver 24V
- When in Emergency stop: outputs deliver 0V
- If pins are unequally set for more than 3 seconds, the safety PLC registers an error and needs to be reset by **restarting the robot**

### **Protective Stop vs Emergency Stop**
The manual distinguishes between:
- **Protective stop**: Robot enters automatically for safety (safety contactors switch off, status lights turn red)
- **Emergency stop**: Enters when Emergency stop button is physically pressed (more severe state)

### **Important Notice (Page 85)**
> **Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.**

### **Troubleshooting Hardware Errors (Page 209)**
When troubleshooting hardware errors, check that:
- The **Emergency stop button is released**
- If the error persists, you may need to turn the robot off and on again to reset components

### **Safety Requirements (Page 36)**
Personnel must be trained on how to engage the Emergency stop function in emergency situations to prevent injury if robots malfunction.

---

**Source**: MiR250 User Guide (v.1.1, 07/2020) — accessed via HandoverDocumentation submodel in the AAS repository.

Based on the MiR250 operator manual documentation, here's what it says about emergency stops:

## Emergency Stop Information (MiR250 Manual)

### **What is Emergency Stop?**
When an Emergency stop button has been physically pressed, the robot enters **Emergency stop** state. When in this state:
- The status light turns **red**
- You cannot move the robot or send it on missions
- The robot must be brought out of Emergency stop to resume operation

### **How to Release Emergency Stop**
To bring the robot out of Emergency stop:
1. **Release the Emergency stop button**
2. **Press the Resume button**

### **Important Warning**
> **NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.

### **Safety System Interface**
The manual also describes the Auxiliary safety function interfaces (Safety A and Safety B) which include:
- **Shared emergency stop in** inputs (inactive = robot goes into emergency stop)
- **Shared E-Stop out** outputs (active when robot is in emergency stop)

### **Troubleshooting**
If the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a safety system component issue, you can identify which component is causing the problem by:
- Signing into the robot interface
- Viewing errors under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**

If you encounter a hardware error and see that the Emergency stop button is not released, you should check that it has been properly released as part of the troubleshooting procedure.

Based on the MiR250 operator manual documentation found in the AAS, here's what it says about emergency stops:

## Emergency Stop Information for MiR250

**Important Notice (Page 85):**
- Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.

**Physical Indicator (Page 18):**
- When the emergency stop is activated, the robot displays a **blinking blue** light indicating it's waiting for a user action to clear the Emergency stop state and acknowledge the change of operating mode.

**Hardware Interface - Safety A (Page 205):**
- Pin 3: "Shared emergency stop in 1" - Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- Pin 6: "Shared emergency stop out 1" - Inactive when the robot is in emergency stop

**Hardware Interface - Safety B (Page 205):**
- Pin 3: "Shared emergency stop in 2" - Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop  
- Pin 6: "Shared E- Stop out 2" - Active when the robot is in emergency stop

**Reset Procedure (Page 209):**
When troubleshooting hardware errors, check that the Emergency stop button is released.

The documentation indicates the robot has both physical E-Stop buttons and programmable safety interface connections (Safety A and Safety B) for integration with external safety systems.

Perfect! I now have comprehensive information about emergency stops from the MiR250 operator manual. Let me provide a complete answer to the user.

According to the MiR250 operator manual found in the AAS documentation, here is what it says about emergency stops:

## Emergency Stop Circuit

The Emergency stop circuit goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and connects to the top module. It is intended that any number of Emergency stop buttons can be connected to the circuit.

**How it works:**
- The circuit uses a 24 V signal that passes through all Emergency stop buttons
- When the circuit is broken (buttons pressed), the robot goes into Emergency stop
- Both input pins must receive 24 V for the robot to operate
- Emergency stop buttons must break the circuit when pressed, sending 0 V to both inputs
- If the circuit or button is installed incorrectly and input signals are not equal, the robot enters **Protective stop** until the circuit is fixed

## Shared Emergency Stop

There is also a **shared emergency stop** function consisting of a shared circuit between the robot and top module, enabling them to trigger each other into an Emergency stop.

## Interface Pins

The **Auxiliary safety function interface** has specific pins for emergency stop:

**Interface A:**
- Pin 3: **Shared emergency stop in 1** (Input) — Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- Pin 6: **Shared emergency stop out 1** (Output) — Inactive when the robot is in emergency stop

**Interface B:**
- Pin 3: **Shared emergency stop in 2** (Input) — Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop  
- Pin 6: **Shared E-Stop out 2** (Output) — Active when the robot is in emergency stop

## Status Indication

When in Emergency stop, the status lights on the robot turn **blue** (blinking) — indicating the robot is waiting for a user action to clear the Emergency stop state.

## Troubleshooting

When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a safety system component, you can identify the issue by signing in to the robot interface and viewing errors under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**.

For more details, see pages 86, 94, 98, and 205 of the MiR250 User Guide (v.1.1, 07/2020).

Based on the search results I've gathered, let me compile the information about emergency stops from the MiR250 operator manual:

---

According to the MiR250 operator manual found in the AAS documentation, here's what it says about emergency stops:

## Emergency Stop Overview

**What is Emergency Stop:**
Emergency stop is a state the robot enters when an Emergency stop button has physically been pressed. When the robot is in Emergency stop, the status light turns red, and you cannot move the robot or send it on missions until you bring the robot out of Emergency stop.

**How Emergency Stop Works:**
- When you press the Emergency stop button, internal safety contactors are switched so the robot's top application and all moving parts do not receive power. You can hear the safety contactors emit audible clicks when they are switched.
- MiR250 has one Emergency stop button and an option to connect additional Emergency stop buttons through the electrical interface.

**NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.

## How to Release Emergency Stop

To bring the robot out of Emergency stop:
1. **Release the Emergency stop button** (the physical button must be released)
2. **Press the Resume button**

The Resume button begins flashing blue after you have released the Emergency stop button. If the robot is in Emergency stop, it will immediately resume an operating state after you press the flashing Resume button.

## Monitoring Emergency Stop Status

In the robot interface, you can monitor the emergency stop status by going to:
**Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop**

Here you can also check if Front scanner cover and Back scanner cover is Clean.

## Additional Information

- When the robot is in Emergency stop, the status lights turn red
- The robot cannot be moved or assigned missions while in Emergency stop state
- The Emergency stop circuit goes through the Auxiliary emergency stop interface
- In the Auxiliary emergency stop interface, pins 2 and 3 deliver 24 V from the safety PLC, and pins 4 and 5 connect to the Emergency stop circuit inputs of the safety PLC

**Source:** MiR250 User Guide (en), Date: 07/2020, Revision: v.1.1, pages 84, 18, 178, 95, 94, 98

Excellent! I found a key MiR250-specific result on page 85. Let me compile the information I've gathered from the MiR250 operator manual about emergency stops.

Based on my search of the MiR250 operator manual in the AAS documentation, here's what it says about emergency stops:

## Emergency Stop Information from MiR250 Operator Manual

**Key points about emergency stops:**

1. **Usage Warning** (Page 85):
   - "Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use."

2. **Safety System Monitoring** (Page 99):
   - When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a component in the safety system, you can identify exactly which component is causing the issue by signing in to the robot interface and viewing the errors displayed under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**.

3. **Maintenance Check** (Page 178):
   - Under "Maintenance tasks" in the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop**, check if **Front scanner cover and Back scanner cover** is Clean.

4. **Safety-related Electrical Interfaces** (Page 86):
   - The MiR250 has **Auxiliary emergency stop and Auxiliary safety function interfaces** that can be used to connect the safety PLC to a top module.
   - Each electrical interface is **redundant**, meaning they use two identical circuits.
   - If one of the circuits fails, the robot enters **Protective stop** until both circuits are working correctly again and the robot is restarted, ensuring safe communication between the top module and robot.

5. **External Parts** (Page 14):
   - The 15 Right top compartment provides access to Safe I/Os and GPIO interface.

**Summary:**
The MiR250 is delivered with a default configuration that enables operation without additional safety equipment, but most applications require one or more extra emergency stop buttons. The robot has redundant safety circuits, and if a safety component fails (like scanner covers getting dirty), it will enter Protective or Emergency stop. Diagnostics can be viewed in the robot interface under Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system.

Excellent! I found MiR250-specific results on pages 36, 37, 87, and 104. Let me compile the emergency stop information from these results.

Based on my search of the MiR250 operator manual, here's what it says about emergency stops:

## Emergency Stop Information from MiR250 Operator Manual

**Key Safety Notice (Page 36):**
> "Personnel operating near the robot must be informed on how to engage the robot's Emergency stop function in emergency situations."

**Emergency Stop Button Notice (Page 85):**
> "Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use."

**Shared Emergency Stop Function (Page 87):**
> "This function consists of a shared circuit between the robot and top module, enabling them to trigger each other into an Emergency stop."

**CAUTION - Personnel Safety (Page 36):**
> "Risk of trapping or injury to personnel if robots malfunction or if personnel enter operating hazard zones. Personnel operating near the robot must be informed on how to engage the robot's Emergency stop function in emergency situations."

**Additional Safety Information:**

1. **Evacuation Zones (Page 116):** The MiR Fleet system includes evacuation zones that can deactivate all robots in case of an emergency situation.

2. **Escape Routes (Page 104):** 
   - There must be at least 0.5 m wide and 2.1 m high pedestrian escape routes on each side of the robot, also in operating hazard zones.

3. **Commissioners Responsibilities (Page 37):** Commissioning personnel must ensure the safety of nearby personnel when the robot is accelerating, braking, and maneuvering, and must mark operating hazard zones.

**Source:** MiR250.pdf - HandoverDocumentation submodel (urn:submodel:mir250:type:handoverdocumentation)

records.query: The transport robot in Hall 4 has a red status light — what does it mean and how do I fix it?

result.query: The transport robot in Hall 4 has a red status light — what does it mean and how do I fix it?
result.response:
I found the transport robot in Hall 4 (MiR100_001), but the AAS graph structure doesn't contain operational status or diagnostic information about the robot's red status light. The graph contains only the hierarchical structure (EntryNode, CRX10iA_001, MiR100_001, UR3e_002) and basic nameplate/facility information.

The robot data here is just a structural representation without:
- Real-time operational status
- Error codes
- Diagnostic data
- Maintenance logs

**What you should do:**

1. **Check the robot's local display/interface** - The red status light on the physical robot typically indicates:
   - Emergency stop activated
   - Fault/error condition
   - Communication loss
   - Battery warning
   - Safety zone violation

2. **Look for the robot's own documentation** - Search for PDF manuals or technical documentation specific to the MiR100 robot that would explain status light meanings.

3. **Contact maintenance** - The contact information in the AAS shows:
   - Email: fm-halle4@dfki.example
   - Phone: +49 0000 000004

Would you like me to search for any documentation that might be associated with other assets, or do you have access to the robot's local interface for more detailed diagnostics?

Now let me compile the answer for the user based on what I've found:

---

**The red status light on the MiR100 transport robot in Hall 4 indicates an Emergency Stop (E-STOP) has been activated.**

## What the red light means:
According to the service request notifications in the AAS system, the red LED is an **Emergency Stop indicator**. When this light is on, the robot has been stopped via the emergency stop mechanism.

## How to fix it:

**Immediate action required:**
1. **Locate and release the emergency stop button** on the robot (typically a large red mushroom-button on the top module or control panel)
2. **Check for obstacles** in the robot's path that may have triggered the E-STOP
3. **Reset the robot** through its control interface after releasing the E-STOP

**Additional checks:**
- Verify no objects are blocking the robot's planned route (page 8 of the manual warns the robot will go around unmapped objects, affecting efficiency)
- Ensure the ambient temperature doesn't exceed 50°C (122°F) to prevent overheating
- Confirm the emergency stop box is properly mounted and accessible

**Contact information:**
- **Manufacturer:** Mobile Industrial Robots
- **Website:** www.mir-robots.com
- **Phone:** +45 20 377 577
- **Email:** support@mir-robots.com
- **Technical contact person:** TechnicalContact (listed in the service request)

The issue is classified as **CorrectiveMaintenance** with **High priority**, so if you're unsure about the procedure, contact the TechnicalContact listed in the service request system.

---

## Summary

Based on the AAS graph data for **MiR100_001** (the transport robot in Hall 4), I found multiple Service Request Notifications indicating the issue:

### What the Red Status Light Means

The red status light indicates one of two conditions:
1. **Emergency stop activated** - The robot's emergency stop has been triggered
2. **Fault light** - The robot is showing a fault condition

Multiple entries in the system confirm: *"Emergency stop triggered - red LED on"* and *"Fault light on transport robot MiR100_001 in Hall 4"*

### How to Fix It

**Immediate Steps:**

1. **Reset the Emergency Stop**
   - Locate the physical emergency stop button on the robot
   - Press and hold the E-stop button to release it
   - The red LED should turn off once reset

2. **Clear the Service Request Notification**
   - The fault has been logged in the AAS system as a Service Request Notification (SRN)
   - This needs to be acknowledged and cleared by maintenance personnel

3. **Power Cycle (if needed)**
   - If the E-stop reset doesn't work, perform a power cycle of the robot
   - Wait for the system to fully boot up before attempting operation

4. **Inspect for Issues**
   - Check for any physical obstructions or safety interlock activations
   - Verify all safety sensors are clear and functional

**Important Notes:**
- The robot (MiR100_001) is currently **immobilized** due to the E-stop activation
- Multiple service requests have been logged in the system tracking this issue
- If the problem persists after reset, contact maintenance for a full diagnostic inspection

The data shows this is a known issue with the transport robot in Hall 4 that requires manual intervention to reset the emergency stop system.

Based on my search of the AAS knowledge graph and MiR100 transport robot documentation, here's what I found:

## About the Red Status Light

**According to the MiR100 Operator Manual (page 14):**

The MiR100 has **yellow-purple error lights** that illuminate when the vehicle "comes to a situation where it cannot find a safe path to its destination." The robot will stop and turn on these error lights.

**Important:** The MiR100 manual does not specifically document a **red** status light as a built-in indicator. The red light you're seeing is likely:

1. **An external emergency stop indicator** (the MiR100 has an emergency stop box that can be mounted on top modules)
2. **A status indicator on the charging station**
3. **A third-party monitoring system indicator**
4. **External safety system indicator**

## Troubleshooting Steps

**If the robot is stuck or showing an error:**

1. **Check for physical obstructions** — Clear any obstacles from the robot's path
2. **Verify sensor coverage** — Ensure laser scanners and cameras are not blocked (Section 8 of manual)
3. **Check safety zones** — Ensure no person or object is in the safety zones (Section 2.4)
4. **Verify charging status** — If at docking station, the emergency stop is activated during charging (page 30)
5. **Check for map updates** — Update maps with areas without drive surfaces like stairways (page 7)

## Recommended Action

**If the red light persists:**

1. **Consult the manufacturer** — Contact Mobile Industrial Robots at support@mir-robots.com or visit www.mir-robots.com
2. **Check Service Request Notifications** — The MiR100_001 AAS has many SRN records that may contain specific error codes and descriptions
3. **Review error codes** — Look at the `ErrorCode` and `ShortText` properties in the ServiceRequestNotification submodels for specific fault information

**General guidance:** A red status light typically indicates a critical fault requiring immediate attention. If this is an emergency stop indicator, reset the E-Stop and investigate the root cause before resuming operation.
result.final_answer:
Based on my investigation of the AAS data and MiR100 documentation, I can now provide a comprehensive answer.

---

Based on my search of the AAS knowledge graph and MiR100 transport robot documentation, here's what I found:

## About the Red Status Light

**According to the MiR100 Operator Manual (page 14):**

The MiR100 has **yellow-purple error lights** that illuminate when the vehicle "comes to a situation where it cannot find a safe path to its destination." The robot will stop and turn on these error lights.

**Important:** The MiR100 manual does not specifically document a **red** status light as a built-in indicator. The red light you're seeing is likely:

1. **An external emergency stop indicator** (the MiR100 has an emergency stop box that can be mounted on top modules)
2. **A status indicator on the charging station**
3. **A third-party monitoring system indicator**
4. **External safety system indicator**

## Troubleshooting Steps

**If the robot is stuck or showing an error:**

1. **Check for physical obstructions** — Clear any obstacles from the robot's path
2. **Verify sensor coverage** — Ensure laser scanners and cameras are not blocked (Section 8 of manual)
3. **Check safety zones** — Ensure no person or object is in the safety zones (Section 2.4)
4. **Verify charging status** — If at docking station, the emergency stop is activated during charging (page 30)
5. **Check for map updates** — Update maps with areas without drive surfaces like stairways (page 7)

## Recommended Action

**If the red light persists:**

1. **Consult the manufacturer** — Contact Mobile Industrial Robots at support@mir-robots.com or visit www.mir-robots.com
2. **Check Service Request Notifications** — The MiR100_001 AAS has many SRN records that may contain specific error codes and descriptions
3. **Review error codes** — Look at the `ErrorCode` and `ShortText` properties in the ServiceRequestNotification submodels for specific fault information

**General guidance:** A red status light typically indicates a critical fault requiring immediate attention. If this is an emergency stop indicator, reset the E-Stop and investigate the root cause before resuming operation.

---

Based on my investigation of the AAS for the transport robot in Hall 4 (MiR100_001), here's what I found:

## Red Status Light Meaning

According to the **MiR100 operator manual** (page 15), the robot has an **"LED color light bar (on four sides)"** that indicates operating status. The manual specifically mentions on **page 14** that when the robot is "lost" (cannot find a safe path), it shows **"yellow-purple error lights."**

While the exact meaning of a **red** light is not explicitly documented in the available PDF, based on standard industrial AGV conventions and the context of the manual:

- **Red status light** typically indicates a **fault, error, or critical condition** requiring attention

## Possible Causes & Troubleshooting Steps

Based on the manual, here are recommended steps to diagnose and fix:

1. **Check the MiR Web Interface** (page 20):
   - Log on to the MiR Web Interface for the vehicle
   - Navigate to **Service > Status > Diagnostics**
   - Check each component's status (should read green "OK")

2. **Common issues that trigger error states:**
   - Robot lost (cannot find path to destination)
   - Battery issues
   - Sensor malfunctions (SICK laser scanners)
   - Communication errors

3. **Immediate actions:**
   - Verify the robot is not in a blocked area
   - Check if emergency stop has been activated
   - Ensure proper mounting of loads (page 7)

4. **If the problem persists:**
   - Contact your retailer for service (page 25)
   - Manufacturer support: Mobile Industrial Robots, +45 20 377 577, support@mir-robots.com

**Note:** The MiR100_001 entity is registered in Hall 4's HierarchicalStructures submodel. If you need to create a service request notification for this issue, I can help with that.

records.query: The transport robot in Hall 4 has a red status light — what does it mean and how do I fix it?

result.query: The transport robot in Hall 4 has a red status light — what does it mean and how do I fix it?
result.response:
I'll help you find information about the transport robot in Hall 4 and its red status light issue.

Let me start by searching for the AAS of the transport robot in Hall 4.









I see - I need to use exact matches for idShort. Let me first get the templates index to understand the structure, then search more effectively.









Good, I can see there's a "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" template which would be relevant for a transport robot. Let me search for AAS with Hall4 or transport-related idShort values using exact matches.









Found it - there's an AAS with idShort "Hall4". Now let me get its submodels to find the transport robot technical data.









Let me check if there are more AAS shells in Hall4 or if the transport robot is in a different shell. Let me search for AGV or transport-related assets more broadly.









I can see there are several transport robots (MiR series - MiR100, MiR250) in the system. Let me search specifically for the transport robot in Hall 4. Based on the results, I can see MiR100_001 and MiR250_001/002. Let me check which one is in Hall 4 by looking at the hierarchical structures or location data.









I see MiR100_001 has Nameplate and many ServiceRequestNotifications but no HierarchicalStructures submodel. Let me check if there's location information in the nameplate or facility information submodels, and also check MiR250_001/002.









Let me check the nameplate of MiR100_001 for location information:









Let me check the hierarchical structures submodel in Hall4 to see if there's a transport robot defined there:









Perfect! I found that MiR100_001 is in the hierarchical structures of Hall4. This is the transport robot in Hall 4. Now let me check if there's a Service Request Notification with information about the red status light. Let me also search for any service request notifications that might be related to a red light or status issue.









Let me search the PDF documentation for information about the red status light. First, I need to get the submodel_id for the MiR100_001 AAS to search its documentation:









Now let me search the documentation for information about the red status light:









The search returned no results with "not_indexed" diagnostic, which means no document chunks are available for this submodel. Let me try searching with the Technical Data AGV submodel from the type AAS (MiR100_Type):

















Let me search more broadly for MiR250 documentation since it also has TechnicalDataAGV:

















The documentation search is not finding indexed chunks. Let me try a different approach - let me check the Handover Documentation submodel which typically contains troubleshooting and manual information:

















Good! I found the MiR100 user guide documentation. Let me search more specifically for status light or indicator information:









I found a relevant result on page 14 about "Alert for 'lost'" which mentions "yellow-purple error lights". Let me search more specifically for "red light" or "beacon" or "fault":









Let me search for "beacon" or "light signal" specifically:









Let me check the troubleshooting section (page 25) more specifically:









I found some troubleshooting information on page 25. Let me also check if there are Service Request Notifications that might indicate an active issue. Let me query the graph for the specific SRN related to MiR100_001:









The query shows there are ServiceRequestNotification submodels but I need to get more details about their contents. Let me search for more specific information about the red light by looking at what the graph contains about status indicators:









Based on my research, I now have enough information to provide a comprehensive answer. Let me compile the findings:

## Summary

I found that the transport robot in Hall 4 is **MiR100_001** (a Mobile Industrial Robots AGV). Based on the MiR100 User Guide documentation found in the AAS:

**According to the MiR100 User Guide (page 14):**
- The robot has **yellow-purple error lights** that activate when the vehicle cannot find a safe path to its destination
- This is the "Alert for 'lost'" condition

**From the troubleshooting section (page 25):**
When MiR shows an error or the vehicle experiences issues:
1. Pull the vehicle to an unobstructed area
2. Check if the destination is blocked
3. Verify the vehicle's destination corresponds with its position on the map
4. Check that the camera is unobstructed and working
5. Check the status in the Diagnostic menu in the MiR Web Interface
6. Ensure wheels are unobstructed and not tangled

**Based on general MiR robot behavior:**
A **red status light** typically indicates a fault or error condition. Common causes include:
- Emergency stop activated
- Sensor obstruction (laser scanner, camera blocked)
- Path planning failure
- Mechanical issues (wheels tangled, overheating)
- Battery or charging issues

**Recommended troubleshooting steps:**
1. Check the MiR Web Interface Diagnostic menu for specific error messages
2. Clear any obstacles around the robot and its path
3. Verify the robot's position matches the map
4. Check that sensors (laser scanner, camera) are unobstructed
5. Check wheel condition and ensure nothing is tangled
6. Verify ambient temperature doesn't exceed 50°C (122°F)
7. Consult the full troubleshooting section in the manual (page 25)

For detailed error code interpretation, please refer to the MiR Web Interface diagnostic menu or contact Mobile Industrial Robots support at support@mir-robots.com.

tool_calls.name: query_aas_graph

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE aas.idShort CONTAINS 'Hall4' OR aas.idShort CONTAINS 'transport'
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId, el.idShort
LIMIT 20

tool_calls.result_preview: {"error":"forbidden_pattern","violations":[{"rule":"idShort_contains_or_regex","hint":"Use exact match instead: WHERE aas.idShort = 'ExactName'. See cypher.md anti-pattern #4.","match":"aas.idShort CO

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE aas.idShort = 'Hall4' OR aas.idShort = 'transport' OR aas.idShort = 'Transport'
RETURN aas.id, aas.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall4'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/HierarchicalStructures/1/1/Submodel'
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id

tool_calls.name: query_aas_graph

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
      -[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
WHERE el.idShort CONTAINS 'location' OR el.idShort CONTAINS 'hall'
RETURN el.idShort, sm.idShort

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})
      -[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.smId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall4'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
      -[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:Entity)
RETURN el.idShort, el.entityType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
      -[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(srn:SubmodelElement)
WHERE srn.idShort = 'ServiceRequestNotification'
RETURN sm.idShort, srn.id, srn.idShort
LIMIT 10

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'Nameplate'
RETURN sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'ServiceRequestNotification'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(srn:SubmodelElement)

result.response:
I need to find the transport robot in Hall 4 and check its documentation for the red status light meaning and troubleshooting steps.

Let me start by searching for the asset and then look up its documentation.









No results with "Hall 4" in the asset ID. Let me try a broader search for transport robots first.









I need to use the correct pattern. Let me first get the templates index to understand how transport robots are structured, then search properly.









I found a relevant template: "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" with semanticId `https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0`. AGVs are automated guided vehicles which are transport robots. Let me search for AAS shells that have this template in their submodels.









I need to use exact match. Let me try a different approach - first find all AAS shells, then check their submodels.









The semanticId matches the relationship, not the submodel id itself. Let me search by the semanticId properly:









Found two type shells for AGVs: MiR250_Type and MiR100_Type. These are type shells. I need to find instance shells. Let me search for instance shells derived from these types, or look for shells with "Hall 4" in their name.

















Now I have three AGV instances: MiR250_001, MiR250_002, and MiR100_001. I need to find which one is in Hall 4. Let me check their location information by searching for the AssetLocation submodel.

MiR250_001 has Nameplate and ServiceRequestNotification submodels. Let me check the Nameplate for location information:









No location info in the nameplate. Let me check all three AGV instances for location info. First, let me get the properties from the TechnicalDataAGV submodels to see if there's location data there:









Let me check what elements are in the TechnicalDataAGV submodels:









Let me check the TypeAndApplicationInformation element for location info:









Let me check what's inside TypeAndApplicationInformation:









Let me try a different approach - search for FacilityInformation submodel which should have location data:









Found Hall4! Now I need to find which AGV is in Hall 4. Let me check the FacilityInformation submodel to see what assets are in Hall 4:









This doesn't show the assets. Let me search for Entity elements that represent assets in Hall 4. Looking at the graph schema, Entity nodes can have REPRESENTS_ASSET relationships. Let me search for all Entity nodes and see if any are linked to Hall 4:









I see MiR100_001 is in Hall 4! Let me verify by checking its REPRESENTS_ASSET relationship:









Perfect! MiR100_001 is the transport robot in Hall 4. Now I need to check its documentation for the red status light meaning. Let me search the AAS documents for this AAS:









The document is not indexed for this submodel. Let me try searching with the AAS ID or check the Type AAS (MiR100_Type) which typically contains the documentation:









Let me search more broadly in the MiR100 Type AAS submodels:









The search returned results for UR3e and UR20 robots (which are collaborative robots, not AGVs). Let me search more specifically for the MiR100 transport robot:









Good! I found a document for MiR100 (see result at page 8: "3. Remove unwanted objects from the floor in the vehicle's area"). Let me search more specifically for status light or LED indicators in the MiR100 documentation:









The search is not returning specific results for MiR100 status lights. Let me try searching the MiR100 handover documentation more directly:









The search is still returning results for other robots. Let me search the MiR100 Type AAS directly with a simpler query:









The search results are not specific to MiR100. Let me check if there are Service Request Notifications on the MiR100_001 instance that might indicate a fault:









Let me check the ServiceRequestNotification submodel structure:









There are many Service Request Notifications on MiR100_001. The graph shows that MiR100_001 is the transport robot in Hall 4. Since the documentation search didn't return specific results for "red status light meaning," let me provide the answer based on general knowledge about AGV status lights and the fact that I couldn't find specific documentation in the AAS.

Based on my analysis:

**The transport robot in Hall 4 is MiR100_001** (an ABB/AGV transport robot).

**Regarding the red status light:**

Unfortunately, I could not find specific documentation in the AAS for the MiR100_001 transport robot that explains the meaning of a red status light. The document store for this asset's TechnicalDataAGV submodel was not indexed with information about status light indicators.

**General guidance for AGV/transport robot red status lights:**

A red status light on an AGV typically indicates one of the following conditions:
- **Fault/Alarm**: A system fault or error condition requiring attention
- **Emergency Stop**: The robot has been stopped due to an emergency
- **Communication Error**: Loss of communication with the control system
- **Battery Low**: Charging required
- **Obstacle Detected**: Path blocked or safety obstruction

**Recommended next steps:**
1. Check the robot's operator interface/teach pendant for any error codes or messages
2. Look for active Service Request Notifications in the system - MiR100_001 has multiple SRNs recorded that may provide more specific fault information
3. Consult the manufacturer's (ABB) operator manual for MiR100 series AGVs
4. Contact facility maintenance if you need immediate assistance

To get more specific information, I would need to search the actual PDF documentation which may not be properly indexed in the current document store, or check if there are error codes recorded in the Service Request Notifications on this asset.

---

## Summary for the MiR100 Transport Robot in Hall 4

I've identified your **MiR100_001** transport robot in Hall 4 and searched its documentation for information about the red status light.

### What I found in the documentation:

According to the MiR100 operator manual (page 14), when the vehicle **cannot find a safe path to its destination** (the "lost" condition), it:
- **Stops** 
- **Turns on yellow-purple error lights**

### What I couldn't find:

The available PDF documentation doesn't contain specific information about a **red status light** or its meaning. The documentation mentions:
- Yellow-purple error lights for the "lost" condition
- No explicit error code table with red light indicators

### Recommended next steps:

1. **Check the robot's display screen** — MiR robots typically show error messages on their display that indicate the specific fault
2. **Consult the MiR Support site** — The manual references:
   - Scan the QR code for support
   - Visit www.mir-robots.com
   - Contact: +45 20 377 577 or support@mir-robots.com
3. **Check for physical error code displays** — The robot may have LED patterns or error codes that correspond to specific faults

### General MiR100 troubleshooting guidance:
- If the robot is "lost" (yellow-purple lights): Clear the path, ensure the map is up to date, or manually guide it back to a known location
- Check that charging station is accessible if battery-related
- Verify no obstacles are blocking the planned route

Would you like me to create a Service Request Notification for this robot to track the fault, or do you have additional details about the red light (e.g., when it appears, any error messages on the display)?

result.response:

Let me start by finding the transport robot asset in Hall 4.

Let me fix the query.









Let me get the details of one of these service request notifications to see if any indicate a fault with a red light.









The values aren't showing up directly. Let me try a different approach - search for properties with values in the SRN.









Found it! There's an open service request notification with error code "Emergency Stop" and high priority. This likely explains the red status light. Let me check if there are more SRNs that might give additional context.









Let me try a different query structure.









Excellent! I found multiple service request notifications with error codes like "FAULT_LIGHT", "E-STOP-RED-LED", "Emergency Stop - Red LED", and "emergency-stop-red-led". Let me get more details about one of the FAULT_LIGHT errors to understand what the fix should be.









Let me get the long text or detailed information for this SRN to understand what the fault is.









Let me check the Nameplate submodel for technical information about the robot's status lights.









The values aren't populated. Let me try searching the PDF documentation with different terms to find information about the red status light.

The template name is "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles". Let me get that template.









Based on my investigation, I can now provide you with an answer. The AAS graph shows multiple Service Request Notifications (SRNs) for the MiR100_001 transport robot in Hall 4 with error codes related to red lights and emergency stops.

## Summary

**The red status light on your MiR100 transport robot in Hall 4 indicates an E-STOP (Emergency Stop) or FAULT_LIGHT condition.**

According to the service request notifications stored in the AAS:
- **Error codes found**: "FAULT_LIGHT", "E-STOP-RED-LED", "Emergency Stop - Red LED", "emergency-stop-red-led"
- **Service type**: CorrectiveMaintenance (high or medium priority)
- **Status**: Open

**What this means:**
The red status light is illuminated because the robot has detected an emergency stop condition or a fault with the light system itself. This is a safety-related alarm that requires attention before the robot can resume normal operation.

**How to fix it:**
1. **Check the physical E-STOP button** on the robot - ensure it hasn't been triggered
2. **Inspect safety sensors and interlocks** - check if any safety features have been activated
3. **Review the robot's control panel** for additional error messages
4. **Reset the emergency stop** if safe to do so (pull out the E-STOP button)
5. **Contact TechnicalContact** (as indicated in the SRN) for assistance if the issue persists

The documentation PDF (MiR100.pdf) exists in the system but isn't currently indexed for text search. For detailed troubleshooting steps, refer to the manufacturer's MiR100 manual or contact technical support.

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
      -[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(e:Entity)
WHERE e.entityType CONTAINS 'Robot' OR e.entityType CONTAINS 'transport'
OPTIONAL MATCH (e)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
WHERE asset.assetKind = 'Instance'
OPTIONAL MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_DISPLAY_NAME]->(ds:LangString {language: 'en'})
WHERE ds.text CONTAINS 'Hall 4' OR ds.text CONTAINS 'Hall4'
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, e.idShort, e.entityType, asset.globalAssetId, ds.text AS displayName

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
      -[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(e:Entity)
OPTIONAL MATCH (e)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
OPTIONAL MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_DESCRIPTION]->(desc:LangString)
WHERE desc.text CONTAINS 'Hall 4' OR desc.text CONTAINS 'Hall4'
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, e.idShort, e.entityType, asset.globalAssetId

MATCH (srn:SubmodelElement {smId: 'urn:aas-hybrid-mcp:srn:001:e50b22f5'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (srn)-[:HAS_VALUE]->(val)
RETURN srn.idShort, val.value AS shortText

MATCH (srn:SubmodelElement {smId: 'urn:aas-hybrid-mcp:srn:001:e50b22f5'})
MATCH (srn)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(prop:Property)
RETURN prop.idShort, prop.value

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:mir100:001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'ServiceRequestNotification'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(srn:SubmodelElement)
MATCH (srn)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(prop:Property)
WHERE prop.idShort = 'ErrorCode'
RETURN srn.idShort, prop.value AS errorCode, prop.value AS shortText

MATCH (srn:SubmodelElement)
WHERE srn.smId CONTAINS 'srn:001'
MATCH (srn)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(prop:Property)
WHERE prop.idShort = 'ErrorCode'

MATCH (srn:SubmodelElement)
WHERE srn.smId = 'urn:aas-hybrid-mcp:srn:001:194a86a0'
MATCH (srn)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(prop:Property)

MATCH (srn:SubmodelElement)
WHERE srn.smId = 'urn:aas-hybrid-mcp:srn:001:194a86a0'
MATCH (srn)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(prop:Property)
WHERE prop.idShort IN ['LongText', 'DetailedInformation', 'ShortText']
RETURN prop.idShort, prop.value
